questions.rationale: Vasovagal syncope has three hallmarks: a trigger (prolonged standing, heat, emotional stress), a prodrome (nausea, diaphoresis, visual dimming, pallor), and rapid complete recovery without post-ictal confusion. This presentation fits all three criteria. Seizure involves tonic-clonic movements preceding LOC with post-ictal confusion. Cardiac arrhythmia causes abrupt LOC without prodrome. Orthostatic hypotension occurs on standing from recumbent, not after prolonged standing.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old male experienced sudden loss of consciousness while climbing stairs. His wife reports he had no warning symptoms. He recovered completely within 1 minute. On exam: a harsh grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur is heard best at the right upper sternal border with radiation to the carotid arteries. Carotid upstrokes are diminished bilaterally (pulsus parvus et tardus). What is the most likely underlying cause of his syncope?

options.A: Vasovagal syncope from exertion-related hypotension
options.B: Severe aortic stenosis causing exertional syncope
options.C: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with dynamic outflow obstruction
options.D: Vertebrobasilar insufficiency from cervical spondylosis

questions.rationale: Exertional syncope in an older adult with a harsh systolic ejection murmur at the right upper sternal border radiating to the carotids, pulsus parvus et tardus (slow-rising diminished carotid pulses), is classic for severe aortic stenosis. AS causes syncope by failing to increase cardiac output with exertion, producing cerebral hypoperfusion. Syncope is one of the classic triad of severe AS symptoms (syncope, angina, dyspnea) and carries a median survival of 3 years without valve replacement.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old male soccer player has an episode of sudden loss of consciousness during a sprint. He had no prodrome. Family history is significant for a brother who died suddenly at age 19 during a basketball game. On exam: a systolic murmur is heard that increases with Valsalva and decreases with squatting. ECG shows LVH with deep Q waves in the lateral leads. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Vasovagal syncope from dehydration during exercise
options.B: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HOCM)
options.C: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome with SVT
options.D: Commotio cordis from a prior chest impact

questions.rationale: HOCM is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes. The dynamic outflow tract obstruction murmur that increases with Valsalva (reduced preload worsens obstruction) and decreases with squatting (increased preload reduces obstruction) is pathognomonic. LVH with deep septal Q waves on ECG and a family history of sudden cardiac death confirm this diagnosis. All first-degree relatives require screening echocardiography.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old female with hypertension and heart failure presents after fainting when she stood up quickly from a chair. No prodrome occurred. She was alert immediately after the event. Her medications include lisinopril, carvedilol, and furosemide, all started within the past 2 months. Lying BP: 138/82, HR 78. Standing BP after 2 minutes: 106/64, HR 82. Which diagnosis does this BP response confirm?

options.A: Vasovagal syncope from standing
options.B: Orthostatic hypotension (drop in SBP ≥20 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing)
options.C: Cardiac arrhythmia producing hemodynamic compromise
options.D: Hyperventilation-induced syncope

questions.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as a sustained drop in systolic BP ≥20 mmHg or diastolic BP ≥10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing. This patient's BP dropped from 138/82 to 106/64 (SBP drop of 32 mmHg) without a compensatory rise in heart rate (HR only 78 — suggesting autonomic dysfunction or beta-blocker blunting). Multiple antihypertensives and a diuretic in a volume-sensitive heart failure patient create high risk for orthostatic hypotension. Medication adjustment is the primary intervention.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old female is brought in after a witnessed episode of loss of consciousness preceded by a blank stare, followed by generalized shaking of all extremities lasting 90 seconds. She was confused and drowsy for 30 minutes afterward. She bit her tongue and had urinary incontinence. Witnesses confirm the shaking began BEFORE she lost consciousness. ECG is normal. What is the most important differentiation from true syncope?

options.A: Urinary incontinence cannot occur in syncope, confirming seizure
options.B: Tonic-clonic movements preceding loss of consciousness, post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, and prolonged recovery identify this as a seizure, not syncope
options.C: The normal ECG confirms this cannot be cardiac syncope
options.D: Duration of shaking >30 seconds rules out convulsive syncope

questions.rationale: The key clinical distinction: in syncope, brief convulsive movements (convulsive syncope) occur AFTER LOC due to cerebral hypoperfusion, lasting <15 seconds, with rapid full recovery. In seizures, tonic-clonic activity precedes or coincides with LOC, lasts 1–3 minutes, and is followed by post-ictal confusion lasting minutes to hours. Tongue biting is more common in seizure; urinary incontinence can occur in both. ECG should still be performed to exclude cardiac cause for any LOC event.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old male presents after a witnessed sudden collapse while walking to his car. No prodrome. He regained consciousness after about 1 minute. This is his second such episode in 2 months. ECG shows: P waves present, PR interval progressively prolonging before a non-conducted P wave, then the cycle resets (R-R intervals gradually shortening then a pause). HR 44. What is the most likely cause of his syncope?

options.A: First-degree AV block — a benign finding
options.B: Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block causing intermittent syncope
options.C: Mobitz type II second-degree AV block requiring pacemaker
options.D: Third-degree (complete) heart block

questions.rationale: Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) AV block is characterized by progressive PR prolongation before a dropped beat (non-conducted P wave), then the cycle resets — this is the ECG described. While typically considered benign, symptomatic Wenckebach causing recurrent syncope in an elderly patient requires cardiology evaluation and consideration of pacemaker placement. Mobitz type II has consistent PR intervals with sudden dropped beats and carries a higher risk of progression to complete heart block.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old male with T1DM presents after a witnessed syncopal episode at work. Coworkers reported he was pale and sweaty before collapsing. He regained consciousness after receiving juice from a colleague. Blood glucose in the office is now 68 mg/dL (was reportedly 42 mg/dL at the time of the event per emergency glucometer). He takes insulin glargine and aspart. Which mechanism caused his syncope?

options.A: Cardiac arrhythmia from hypoglycemia-induced QT prolongation
options.B: Cerebral hypoperfusion from hypoglycemia causing loss of consciousness
options.C: Vasovagal response to the pain of insulin injection
options.D: Diabetic neuropathy causing autonomic orthostatic hypotension

questions.rationale: Severe hypoglycemia (<54 mg/dL with symptoms; <40 mg/dL with LOC) causes cerebral glucose deprivation — the brain's exclusive fuel — leading to progressive neurological dysfunction from confusion to seizure to coma. The adrenergic prodrome (diaphoresis, pallor, tremor, tachycardia) preceded loss of consciousness. This is not cardiac syncope but metabolic LOC. Rapid glucose restoration (oral glucose if responsive, glucagon IM if unresponsive) reverses the episode. Review of insulin regimen and hypoglycemia unawareness risk is the management priority.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old male presents after a sudden LOC episode while playing basketball. He had brief palpitations immediately before collapsing. ECG on arrival shows sinus rhythm with a QTc of 510 ms (normal <450 ms in men). His sister reportedly had a similar episode. Family history is notable for an uncle who died suddenly at age 32. Electrolytes are normal. What is the most likely cause and appropriate next step?

options.A: QT prolongation from hypokalemia; replete potassium and discharge
options.B: Congenital long QT syndrome with risk of torsades de pointes; urgent cardiology referral and activity restriction
options.C: Athlete's heart with physiological QT adaptation; reassure and continue sports
options.D: Medication-induced QT prolongation; review medication list before further evaluation

questions.rationale: A QTc of 510 ms with exertional syncope, family history of sudden cardiac death, and a sibling with similar episodes is highly concerning for congenital long QT syndrome (LQTS). Torsades de pointes — a polymorphic VT that can degenerate to ventricular fibrillation — is precipitated by adrenergic stimulation (exercise, stress), explaining the exertional setting. Urgent cardiology referral, activity restriction pending evaluation, and avoidance of QT-prolonging medications are immediate steps. Normal electrolytes exclude acquired QT prolongation.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old male presents after an episode of feeling that he was about to faint, associated with the most severe headache of his life that began abruptly. He did not lose consciousness but felt extremely unwell for several minutes. The headache has persisted. Vital signs: BP 168/98, HR 94, neurological exam currently normal. What is the priority diagnosis to exclude?

options.A: Hypertensive urgency causing presyncope
options.B: Subarachnoid hemorrhage presenting as presyncope
options.C: Vasovagal syncope with associated headache
options.D: Orthostatic hypotension from antihypertensive over-medication

questions.rationale: Subarachnoid hemorrhage can present as presyncope rather than frank syncope — the sudden headache of maximal intensity at onset is the critical feature that distinguishes this from benign presyncope causes. Presyncope + thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise. Non-contrast CT head must be performed emergently; if negative after 6 hours of symptom onset, lumbar puncture for xanthochromia is mandatory. The associated hypertension is likely a stress/Cushing response, not the primary diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old healthy female reports losing consciousness while urinating in the middle of the night. Her partner reports she was out for 15–20 seconds and was immediately alert and normal afterward. She had no prodrome. ECG, echocardiogram, and Holter monitor are all normal. She denies any other symptoms. This is her first episode. What type of syncope does this represent?

options.A: Cardiac arrhythmia requiring further electrophysiological study
options.B: Situational syncope (micturition syncope)
options.C: Psychogenic pseudosyncope
options.D: Seizure occurring from sleep

questions.rationale: Situational syncope encompasses reflex syncope triggered by specific activities: coughing, micturition, defecation, swallowing, or laughing. Micturition syncope occurs from a vagal response to bladder emptying, often at night when standing quickly from a warm bed. It is most common in men but occurs in women. Despite the lack of typical prodrome, complete cardiac workup (normal in this case) excludes serious causes, and the situational trigger confirms the diagnosis. Counseling includes sitting down to urinate at night.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old female started venlafaxine (SNRI) 6 weeks ago for GAD. She presents after fainting when she stood up from bed this morning. She has been nauseous (a side effect of the medication) and has had poor oral intake for 3 days. Lying HR 88, BP 118/76; Standing HR 118, BP 88/58 (after 2 minutes). She is alert, without neurological deficits. What combination of factors most likely explains this syncope?

options.A: SNRI-induced QT prolongation causing ventricular arrhythmia
options.B: Venlafaxine-induced orthostatic hypotension plus volume depletion from reduced oral intake
options.C: Serotonin syndrome causing vasodilation and hypotension
options.D: Vasovagal syncope from medication anxiety

questions.rationale: SNRIs (particularly venlafaxine at higher doses) can cause orthostatic hypotension through norepinephrine reuptake inhibition affecting vascular tone regulation. Combined with volume depletion from nausea and poor oral intake (diuretic effect of reduced fluid intake), this patient's compensatory tachycardia cannot maintain blood pressure on standing — producing orthostatic syncope. The positive orthostatic vitals (SBP drop 30 mmHg with reflex tachycardia) confirm the diagnosis. Oral hydration and dose reassessment are appropriate interventions.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old male presents after his second episode of sudden brief LOC, both occurring at rest without any trigger or prodrome. He has no cardiac symptoms otherwise. ECG shows a right bundle branch block pattern with coved-type ST elevation of 3 mm in V1–V2. Echo is normal. Electrolytes are normal. The pattern normalizes when leads are moved one intercostal space higher. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Reassure him that RBBB is a benign finding; no further workup needed
options.B: Prescribe metoprolol for presumed arrhythmia management
options.C: Urgent electrophysiology referral for Brugada syndrome evaluation
options.D: Repeat ECG in 3 months to monitor for changes

questions.rationale: Brugada syndrome is characterized by the Type 1 Brugada pattern on ECG (coved-type ST elevation ≥2 mm in V1–V2) in the absence of structural heart disease, associated with ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death, predominantly at rest or during sleep. Two syncopal episodes without prodrome confirm symptomatic Brugada syndrome. Urgent referral to electrophysiology is required — ICD implantation is the definitive management for symptomatic disease. Beta-blockers are not appropriate; they can worsen arrhythmias in Brugada syndrome.
